title: Hajj 2026: The Complete Guide — Dates, Rituals, Requirements & Spiritual Preparation
excerpt: Everything you need to know about Hajj 2026 in one place: the confirmed dates, every ritual explained step by step, eligibility requirements, what to pack, spiritual preparation, and the profound meaning behind every act of the pilgrimage.
author: QuranBookk
content:
## The Most Extraordinary Journey a Human Being Can Make

Every year, approximately two million Muslims from every nation on earth converge on one valley in the Arabian Peninsula. They wear the same two sheets of white cloth. They speak one phrase in one language. For five days, there is no king and no beggar — just human beings standing equally before their Creator.

This is Hajj.

Allah commanded it directly in the Quran:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَأَذِّن فِى ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا وَعَلَىٰ كُلِّ ضَامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِن كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ"
  translation="And proclaim to the people the Hajj; they will come to you on foot and on every lean camel; they will come from every distant pass."
  reference="Surah Al-Hajj 22:27"
/>

This verse was addressed to Ibrahim ﷺ — and his call, made over 4,000 years ago in a desert valley, continues to be answered. Every Hajj, every year, is a direct response to that ancient proclamation.

This guide covers everything: the 2026 dates, every ritual in precise sequence with its spiritual meaning, eligibility and requirements, practical preparation, and what the journey will do to you spiritually. Whether you are planning to go this year, preparing for future years, or seeking to understand the greatest pilgrimage on earth — this is the guide you need.

---

## Part 1: Hajj 2026 — Dates, Facts & Context

### When Is Hajj 2026?

Hajj 2026 corresponds to **8–13 Dhul Hijjah 1447 AH**. Based on current astronomical calculations and confirmed by multiple Islamic calendar authorities:

| Day | Hijri Date | Expected Gregorian Date |
|---|---|---|
| Yawm al-Tarwiyah | 8 Dhul Hijjah | Mon 25 May 2026 |
| Day of Arafah | 9 Dhul Hijjah | Tue 26 May 2026 |
| Eid al-Adha | 10 Dhul Hijjah | Wed 27 May 2026 |
| First Tashreeq Day | 11 Dhul Hijjah | Thu 28 May 2026 |
| Second Tashreeq Day | 12 Dhul Hijjah | Fri 29 May 2026 |
| Final Tashreeq Day | 13 Dhul Hijjah | Sat 30 May 2026 |

<Callout type="note">
All Hajj dates depend on the official moon sighting announced by Saudi Arabian authorities. Dates may shift by one day. Always confirm with official Saudi sources or your licensed Hajj operator closer to the season.
</Callout>

### Scale and Significance

Hajj is the largest annual peaceful gathering of human beings on earth. In 2024, approximately 1.8 million pilgrims from over 180 countries performed it. The word *Hajj* (حَجّ) appears 12 times in the Quran. An entire surah — Surah Al-Hajj (22) — is named after it.

The Prophet ﷺ performed Hajj only once in his life — in the 10th year after Hijra, just three months before his death. This was his Farewell Pilgrimage, during which he delivered the most important address in Islamic history and declared the religion complete.

<AyahBlock
  arabic="ٱلْيَوْمَ أَكْمَلْتُ لَكُمْ دِينَكُمْ وَأَتْمَمْتُ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعْمَتِى وَرَضِيتُ لَكُمُ ٱلْإِسْلَٰمَ دِينًا"
  translation="This day I have perfected for you your religion and completed My favour upon you and have approved for you Islam as religion."
  reference="Surah Al-Ma'idah 5:3 — revealed on the Day of Arafah during the Farewell Hajj"
/>

---

## Part 2: Who Must Perform Hajj — Conditions and Eligibility

Hajj is obligatory once in a lifetime upon every Muslim who meets all five conditions simultaneously. This is one of the most important principles: the obligation only applies when *all* conditions are met together.

### The Five Conditions of Obligation

**1. Islam** — Hajj is obligatory only on Muslims.

**2. Maturity (Bulugh)** — Hajj is not obligatory on children. However, if a child performs Hajj, it is valid and rewarded — but they must still perform it again after maturity. The Prophet ﷺ confirmed that a child's Hajj counts as worship but does not fulfil the adult obligation.

**3. Sound Mind (Aql)** — Those who have permanently lost their mental capacity are not obligated.

**4. Physical Ability (Istitatah al-Badaniyyah)** — The person must be physically capable of making the journey. Chronic illness, extreme old age, or disability that makes travel impossible removes the obligation. In such cases, a person may send a proxy (*Hajj badal* or *Hajj al-niyabah*) if they have the financial means.

**5. Financial Ability (Istitatah al-Maliyyah)** — The person must be able to afford the journey *after* meeting all their obligations: debts paid, family provided for during the absence. Going into serious debt to perform Hajj is not the sunnah.

### The Condition for Women: The Mahram

Classically, a woman was required to be accompanied by her mahram (husband or male relative she cannot marry). However, **as of 2021, Saudi Arabia permits women to perform Hajj without a mahram, provided they travel as part of an organised women's group**. This policy was confirmed for subsequent seasons including 2025 and 2026.

Women aged 45 and above may travel in an organised group without a mahram. Women under 45 should confirm their country's official position with their licensed Hajj operator, as some national authorities still apply the mahram condition.

### Special Notes for Hajj 2026

Based on policies established in 2025 and expected to continue:
- **Children under 12** are not permitted to accompany pilgrims per Saudi Ministry regulations
- **First-time pilgrims are given priority** over those who have performed Hajj before
- **Pilgrims must not have performed Hajj in the past five years** (domestic pilgrims; Saudi residents and citizens)
- All registration goes through the official **Nusuk platform** (nusuk.sa)

---

## Part 3: The Three Types of Hajj

Every pilgrim must choose one of three valid forms of Hajj before entering the state of Ihram. This is not optional — the choice determines what rituals follow and whether an *udhiyah* (sacrifice) is required.

### 1. Hajj al-Ifrad (Hajj Only)

The pilgrim enters Ihram specifically for Hajj, performs only the Hajj rituals, and does not combine with Umrah. No sacrificial animal is required. This form is the simplest and was the default practice of many Companions.

### 2. Hajj al-Qiran (Combined — Hajj and Umrah Together)

The pilgrim enters a single Ihram with the intention of performing both Umrah and Hajj together, without releasing Ihram between the two. A sacrificial animal (*hadiy*) is obligatory. This was the form chosen by the Prophet ﷺ in his Farewell Hajj, according to the strongest narration.

### 3. Hajj al-Tamattu' (Umrah First, Then Hajj) — Most Common for Overseas Pilgrims

The pilgrim performs Umrah first (entering Ihram for Umrah, completing it, releasing Ihram), then re-enters Ihram for Hajj on the 8th of Dhul Hijjah. A sacrificial animal is required. This is the form **recommended by the Prophet ﷺ for those travelling from distant lands** and is the most widely practiced form today.

<Callout type="tip">
For pilgrims travelling from outside Saudi Arabia, Hajj al-Tamattu is the standard recommendation. Confirm with your Hajj operator and follow your madhab's guidance on which form to choose.
</Callout>

---

## Part 4: The Complete Hajj Rituals — Step by Step

The rituals of Hajj follow a precise sequence. Every act connects to the heritage of Ibrahim ﷺ, Hajar, Ismail ﷺ, and the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Understanding the history behind each act transforms Hajj from a series of physical movements into a living conversation with the history of monotheism.

### Step 1: Ihram — The Sacred State (8 Dhul Hijjah)

**What it is**: Ihram is both a physical and spiritual state of consecration. It involves wearing the Ihram garments and making the formal intention (*niyyah*) to begin Hajj.

**The Ihram garments**:
- **Men**: Two unstitched white sheets — one wrapped around the waist (*izar*), one draped over the left shoulder (*rida'*). No sewn clothing is permitted. No head covering.
- **Women**: Regular modest Islamic clothing. Women must not cover their faces with niqab during Ihram according to the majority scholarly position, nor wear gloves. The head must be covered.

**The Miqat**: Ihram must be entered at or before the Miqat — the designated boundary stations surrounding Makkah. There are five Miqat stations, one for each direction of approach. Pilgrims arriving by air will pass over a Miqat; they must be in Ihram before crossing it. Entering Makkah without Ihram when intending Hajj is a violation requiring a *dam* (compensatory sacrifice).

**What is prohibited in Ihram**: cutting hair or nails, using perfume, hunting animals, sexual relations, marriage contracts, and for men, covering the head or wearing sewn clothing.

**The Talbiyah**: From the moment of entering Ihram until the first stone is thrown at Jamarat, the pilgrim continuously repeats:

*لَبَّيْكَ اللَّهُمَّ لَبَّيْكَ، لَبَّيْكَ لَا شَرِيكَ لَكَ لَبَّيْكَ، إِنَّ الْحَمْدَ وَالنِّعْمَةَ لَكَ وَالْمُلْكَ، لَا شَرِيكَ لَكَ*

*Labbayk Allahumma labbayk. Labbayk la sharika laka labbayk. Innal hamda wan-ni'mata laka wal-mulk. La sharika lak.*

*Here I am, O Allah, here I am. Here I am, You have no partner, here I am. Verily all praise, grace, and sovereignty belong to You. You have no partner.*

This is the Hajj's defining expression — the response to Ibrahim's ﷺ ancient call, repeated by every pilgrim, in every language background, in the same Arabic words.

---

### Step 2: Tawaf al-Qudum — The Arrival Circumambulation

Upon arriving in Makkah (for those performing Ifrad or Qiran), the first act is Tawaf al-Qudum — the circumambulation of the Kaaba as a greeting.

**What is the Kaaba?**

The Kaaba is the ancient stone structure at the centre of Masjid al-Haram. It is not worshipped — it is the direction of worship and the symbol of Allah's house on earth. Ibrahim ﷺ and Ismail ﷺ built it on a foundation that had existed since before them, and possibly since the time of Adam ﷺ:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِى بِبَكَّةَ مُبَارَكًا وَهُدًى لِّلْعَٰلَمِينَ"
  translation="Indeed, the first House established for mankind was that at Bakkah — blessed and a guidance for the worlds."
  reference="Surah Ali Imran 3:96"
/>

**How Tawaf is performed**:
- Begin at the Black Stone (*Hajar al-Aswad*), with the Kaaba on your left
- Circle the Kaaba seven times in an **anticlockwise** direction
- During the first three circuits, men perform *ramal* — a brisk, short-stepped walk showing strength, established by the Prophet ﷺ
- Upon passing the Black Stone each circuit, say *Allahu Akbar* and kiss it if possible; if not, gesture toward it
- Continuously make dua and dhikr throughout
- Conclude with two rak'ahs of prayer behind Maqam Ibrahim

**The Black Stone (*Hajar al-Aswad*)**:

It is a stone from Paradise, placed in the corner of the Kaaba. The Prophet ﷺ said it descended white and was turned black by the sins of humanity. It is kissed as a sunnah, following the Prophet ﷺ — not because it can benefit or harm. Umar ibn al-Khattab (رضي الله عنه) famously said while kissing it: *I know that you are a stone that does not benefit or harm. If I had not seen the Prophet ﷺ kiss you, I would not have kissed you.* (Sahih al-Bukhari 1597)

---

### Step 3: Sa'i — Walking Between Safa and Marwah

**What it is**: Walking seven times between the two hills of Safa and Marwah, which are now enclosed within the Masjid al-Haram complex.

**The story**: When Ibrahim ﷺ left Hajar and the infant Ismail ﷺ alone in the waterless valley of Makkah at Allah's command, Hajar ran between the two hills searching desperately for water for her dying son. She ran seven times. When she returned to Ismail ﷺ, water was springing from beneath his feet — this is the spring of Zamzam, still flowing today.

Every act of Sa'i is a re-enactment of a mother's desperate love and trust in Allah. It was so beloved to Allah that He made it an eternal ritual of Hajj and Umrah:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="إِنَّ ٱلصَّفَا وَٱلْمَرْوَةَ مِن شَعَآئِرِ ٱللَّهِ"
  translation="Indeed, Safa and Marwah are among the symbols of Allah."
  reference="Surah Al-Baqarah 2:158"
/>

**How Sa'i is performed**:
- Begin at Safa, facing the Kaaba, and say: *Inna as-Safa wal-Marwaha min sha'a'irillah* (Indeed, Safa and Marwah are among the symbols of Allah)
- Walk toward Marwah — men jog between the two green markers
- Reach Marwah: this completes one circuit
- Return to Safa: this is circuit two
- Repeat until seven circuits are complete, ending at Marwah
- Make dua throughout — this is one of the great occasions for supplications

**Drink Zamzam water** — the miraculous water that has been flowing for over 4,000 years. The Prophet ﷺ said: *Zamzam water is for whatever it is drunk for.* (Sunan Ibn Majah — authenticated by al-Albani) Drink it with intention.

---

### Step 4: Travel to Mina — Yawm al-Tarwiyah (8 Dhul Hijjah)

On the 8th of Dhul Hijjah, pilgrims travel approximately 8 kilometres from Makkah to **Mina** — a vast tent city that exists only for Hajj. They spend the entire day and night there.

Mina is where pilgrims sleep in the massive climate-controlled tent camps provided by Saudi authorities. The name *Tarwiyah* (watering) refers to the ancient custom of pilgrims drawing and carrying water on this day in preparation for the journey ahead.

Spend the night in Mina in prayer, Quran recitation, dhikr, and rest. The following day is the greatest day of the year.

---

### Step 5: Wuquf at Arafah — The Pinnacle of Hajj (9 Dhul Hijjah)

This is the heart of Hajj. Without standing at Arafah, Hajj is invalid.

<HadithBlock
  text="Hajj is Arafah."
  narrator="Abdur-Rahman ibn Ya'mar al-Dili (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sunan Abi Dawud 1949, Sunan al-Tirmidhi 889 — Authenticated"
/>

**What happens at Arafah**: Pilgrims gather on the vast plain and hill of Arafah from after midday until sunset. They stand — or sit, or pray, or weep — in supplication to Allah. No specific prayer is required; no specific words are mandated. The requirement is simply to be there, present, and directed toward Allah.

The Prophet ﷺ described this day:

<HadithBlock
  text="There is no day on which Allah frees more people from the Fire than the Day of Arafah. He draws near and then boasts about them to the angels, saying: What do these people want?"
  narrator="Aisha (رضي الله عنها)"
  source="Sahih Muslim 1348"
/>

**The best dua for Arafah**, taught by the Prophet ﷺ:

*لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا اللَّهُ وَحْدَهُ لَا شَرِيكَ لَهُ، لَهُ الْمُلْكُ وَلَهُ الْحَمْدُ وَهُوَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ*

*La ilaha illallahu wahdahu la sharika lahu, lahul mulku wa lahul hamdu wa huwa ala kulli shayin qadir.*

*There is no god but Allah alone, with no partner. To Him belongs the kingdom and all praise, and He is able to do all things.*

**The Khutbah and Prayers**: A sermon is delivered at Arafah. Dhuhr and Asr prayers are combined and shortened at Dhuhr time — two rak'ahs each — following the Prophet's ﷺ practice.

**For Muslims NOT performing Hajj**: The Day of Arafah is the greatest fasting day of the year.

<HadithBlock
  text="Fasting on the Day of Arafah — I hope from Allah that it expiates the sins of the year before it and the year after it."
  narrator="Abu Qatadah (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih Muslim 1162"
/>

**Do not leave Arafah before sunset**. Staying until after sunset is obligatory. Leaving before sunset requires a compensatory sacrifice.

---

### Step 6: Muzdalifah — The Night Under the Sky (Night of 9–10 Dhul Hijjah)

After sunset at Arafah, pilgrims travel to **Muzdalifah** — an open plain between Arafah and Mina, approximately 9 kilometres from Mina.

**What happens at Muzdalifah**:
- Maghrib and Isha prayers are combined here at Isha time — two and three rak'ahs respectively
- Spend the night sleeping under the open sky — this simplicity is profound after the intensity of Arafah
- Collect 49 or 70 small pebbles (approximately the size of a chickpea) for the stoning ritual
- Depart after Fajr prayer toward Mina

*The elderly, ill, and those with them are permitted to leave Muzdalifah after midnight.* This concession is established by the Prophet ﷺ.

---

### Step 7: Ramy al-Jamarat — Stoning the Pillars (10, 11, 12, and optionally 13 Dhul Hijjah)

In Mina, there are three stone pillars (*Jamarat*): the small (Jamarat al-Sughra), the middle (Jamarat al-Wusta), and the large (Jamarat al-Aqabah).

**Day 1 (10 Dhul Hijjah — Eid al-Adha)**: Stone *only* the large pillar (Jamarat al-Aqabah) with **seven pebbles**, saying *Allahu Akbar* with each throw.

**Days 2 and 3 (11 and 12 Dhul Hijjah)**: Stone all *three* pillars with **seven pebbles each** (21 pebbles per day), beginning with the small, then middle, then large.

**Day 4 (13 Dhul Hijjah — optional)**: Those who stay until the 13th stone all three pillars again.

**The spiritual meaning**: Ibrahim ﷺ was commanded by Allah to sacrifice his son Ismail ﷺ. On his way, Shaytan appeared three times to tempt him to abandon Allah's command. Three times, Ibrahim ﷺ drove Shaytan away with stones. Every throw is a declaration: I reject you, shaytan, exactly as Ibrahim ﷺ rejected you.

<Callout type="tip">
The stoning is not about anger or emotion — it is an act of obedience to Allah's command, following Ibrahim ﷺ. The Prophet ﷺ said: Stoning the Jamarat and Sa'i between Safa and Marwah were only instituted to establish the remembrance of Allah. (Sunan Abi Dawud 1888)
</Callout>

---

### Step 8: Nahr — The Sacrifice (10 Dhul Hijjah)

After stoning Jamarat al-Aqabah on Eid day, pilgrims performing Tamattu or Qiran sacrifice an animal (*hadiy*). This sacrifice is an obligation, not a sunnah, for these forms of Hajj.

Today, this is typically handled through official channels — pilgrims purchase a sacrifice voucher and the slaughter is performed by authorised organisations on their behalf. The meat is distributed to the poor.

The sacrifice commemorates Ibrahim's ﷺ willingness to sacrifice Ismail ﷺ — and Allah's merciful substitution of a ram:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَفَدَيْنَٰهُ بِذِبْحٍ عَظِيمٍ"
  translation="And We ransomed him with a great sacrifice."
  reference="Surah As-Saffat 37:107"
/>

---

### Step 9: Halq or Taqsir — Shaving or Shortening the Hair

After the sacrifice, pilgrims shave their heads (*halq*) or shorten their hair (*taqsir*). Shaving is more virtuous for men. Women cut a small amount — approximately a fingertip's length from the tips of their hair.

This act symbolises the end of Ihram restrictions. After this, all Ihram prohibitions are lifted *except* sexual relations (which requires Tawaf al-Ifadah first).

The Prophet ﷺ made dua three times for those who shave their heads, and once for those who shorten. (Sahih al-Bukhari 1728)

---

### Step 10: Tawaf al-Ifadah — The Pillar Tawaf (10 Dhul Hijjah)

Also called *Tawaf al-Ziyarah*, this Tawaf is one of the *arkaan* (essential pillars) of Hajj — without it, Hajj is fundamentally incomplete.

It is performed exactly like Tawaf al-Qudum: seven circuits around the Kaaba, followed by two rak'ahs at Maqam Ibrahim, then Sa'i if not already performed.

After this Tawaf, *all* Ihram restrictions are completely lifted, including sexual relations. The pilgrim may return to normal clothing.

---

### Step 11: The Days of Tashreeq — Remaining in Mina (11–13 Dhul Hijjah)

Pilgrims return to Mina and remain there through the days of Tashreeq, repeating the stoning ritual daily as described above.

These are days of *dhikr*, gratitude, and joy:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فِىٓ أَيَّامٍ مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ"
  translation="And remember Allah during a numbered days."
  reference="Surah Al-Baqarah 2:203"
/>

Pilgrims who wish to leave early may depart after the 12th (sunset), before the stoning on the 13th. Staying the 13th is better and following the Prophet's ﷺ own practice.

---

### Step 12: Tawaf al-Wida — The Farewell Circumambulation

The final act before leaving Makkah is Tawaf al-Wida — the farewell Tawaf. This is obligatory for every pilgrim except Makkan residents.

Performed exactly like other Tawafs: seven circuits, two rak'ahs, then depart. This must be the **last act in Makkah**. If you shop, eat a meal, or do anything significant after Tawaf al-Wida, you should repeat it.

The Prophet ﷺ commanded that no one leave Makkah without the Kaaba being the last thing they see. (Sahih al-Bukhari 1755)

This is the farewell. Turn and look at the Kaaba. You do not know if you will see it again in this life.

---

## Part 5: The Spiritual Architecture of Hajj

Hajj is not a tourism trip to a holy site. Every ritual carries a burden of meaning that, when understood, transforms the pilgrim.

### The Equality of Ihram

When 2 million people wearing identical white cloth stand together on Arafah — the king and the cleaner, the scholar and the illiterate, the Arab and the non-Arab, the rich and the poor — this is the physical preview of the Day of Judgement. The Ihram is not a uniform of equality in this world; it is a preview of the next. On the Day of Judgement, every distinction of this world is stripped away. Hajj rehearses that reality while a person is still alive to act on it.

### Arafah and the Day of Judgement

The scholars say Arafah is the closest this world comes to the Day of Judgement. Millions gathered in one place, under the same sun, stripped of social markers, waiting to be addressed by their Lord. The *Wuquf* (standing) at Arafah mirrors the *Wuquf* on the Day of Resurrection. Those who stand sincerely at Arafah are being rehearsed for that greater standing — and are promised that if they stood sincerely, they will leave as pure as the day they were born:

<HadithBlock
  text="Whoever performs Hajj for the sake of Allah and does not commit any obscenity or wickedness will return as on the day his mother gave birth to him."
  narrator="Abu Hurairah (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih al-Bukhari 1521, Sahih Muslim 1350"
/>

### The Legacy of Ibrahim ﷺ

Every ritual of Hajj traces the outline of Ibrahim's ﷺ life:
- The Kaaba: built by him and Ismail ﷺ
- The Talbiyah: his call responded to across millennia
- Sa'i: the running of Hajar, his wife
- Zamzam: the water Allah sent for his infant son
- The stoning: his rejection of Shaytan
- The sacrifice: his willingness to give everything to Allah
- The Maqam Ibrahim: the stone on which he stood while building the Kaaba, which miraculously bore his footprint

To perform Hajj is to walk in the footsteps of the father of monotheism — literally, physically, in the same locations, performing the same acts, 4,000 years later.

---

## Part 6: Practical Preparation for Hajj 2026

### Documents Required

- Valid passport (at least 6 months beyond travel dates)
- Hajj visa (obtained only through licensed Hajj operators or Nusuk platform)
- Meningococcal Meningitis (ACWY) vaccination certificate — mandatory, must be administered at least 10 days before arrival
- Additional vaccinations as required by Saudi health authorities (influenza, polio, yellow fever for some nationalities)
- Health certificate and travel insurance (mandatory — must include emergency medical coverage and repatriation)

### Physical Preparation

Hajj is physically demanding. Over five days, a pilgrim typically walks 15–25 kilometres through crowds in June heat (Makkah temperatures in late May can reach 38–42°C). Begin physical preparation 2–3 months before departure:

- Walk 5–10 kilometres daily, progressively increasing
- Practice walking in the Ihram garments to build comfort
- Heat acclimatisation — spend time in warmer conditions if possible
- Consult your doctor, especially if you have heart conditions, diabetes, or respiratory conditions
- Carry personal medications with translated prescriptions

### Essential Packing List

**For Men:**
- 2 sets of Ihram (white unsewn cloth)
- Unstitched sandals (no closed-toe shoes in Ihram)
- Belt or safety pins for Ihram
- Travel prayer mat

**For All Pilgrims:**
- Unscented soap, shampoo, deodorant (perfumed products are prohibited in Ihram)
- Reusable water bottle — stay hydrated; heat exhaustion is real
- Electrolyte sachets or dates for energy
- Comfortable walking shoes (for outside Ihram)
- Umbrella or sun hat (men outside Ihram)
- Small backpack for daily essentials
- Paracetamol, antihistamines, blister plasters, antiseptic wipes
- Travel pillow for nights in Mina and Muzdalifah
- Ear plugs and eye mask for sleeping in group environments
- Emergency contact card and group identification
- Zamzam water container
- Small Quran or Quran app for recitation
- Copy of important duas (dua booklet or phone)

<Callout type="tip">
Write your name, country, group number, and emergency contact on a card and keep it on your person at all times. In the crowds of Hajj, getting separated from your group is common. This card has helped countless pilgrims find their way back.
</Callout>

### Spiritual Preparation — The Most Important Part

Physical and logistical preparation is necessary. But no preparation matters more than the preparation of the heart.

**Make sincere tawbah (repentance)** — Clear your account with Allah before you go. Ask forgiveness from people you have wronged. Return money owed. Hajj does not replace the obligation to make restitution to people — it must accompany it.

**Learn the rituals in advance** — Do not leave this for the journey itself. Read a comprehensive Hajj guide (a book like *The Rites of Hajj and Umrah* by Shaykh Nasir al-Din al-Albani, or *A Guide to Hajj, Umrah and Visiting the Prophet's Mosque* by scholars of the Permanent Committee). Watch video guides. Attend pre-Hajj seminars offered by most licensed operators. Arriving in Makkah without knowing what to do next is unnecessary and adds avoidable stress.

**Make dua to go** — Among the most profound duas a Muslim can make is asking Allah to make Hajj possible. Ibrahim ﷺ called humanity to Hajj with Allah's permission — and only those Allah permits will answer. If you are not yet going, ask.

**Write your will (*wasiyya*)** — This is a sunnah before any major journey, not a morbid act but an act of faith and responsibility.

**Seek forgiveness from family** — Leave no outstanding grievances. Begin this journey in a state of reconciliation.

---

## Part 7: For Those Not Performing Hajj — How to Maximise the 10 Days of Dhul Hijjah

If you are not performing Hajj this year, you are not excluded from its blessings. The first ten days of Dhul Hijjah are the greatest days of worship in the entire Islamic year:

<HadithBlock
  text="There are no days on which righteous deeds are more beloved to Allah than these ten days. They said: Not even jihad in the path of Allah? He said: Not even jihad in the path of Allah, except for a man who goes out with his life and wealth and returns with neither."
  narrator="Ibn Abbas (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sahih al-Bukhari 969"
/>

Based on expected 2026 dates:
- **1 Dhul Hijjah**: approximately 18 May 2026 — begin intensified worship
- **9 Dhul Hijjah (Day of Arafah)**: approximately 26 May — fast this day if not on Hajj
- **10 Dhul Hijjah (Eid al-Adha)**: approximately 27 May — do not fast; celebrate, perform Qurbani

**Recommended acts during the 10 days**:
- Fasting (especially the 9th)
- Increased Quran recitation
- Dhikr — particularly *Allahu Akbar, Alhamdulillah, La ilaha illallah, Subhanallah*
- Sadaqah (charity)
- Performing the Udhiyah/Qurbani sacrifice
- Praying all prayers on time and adding voluntary prayers

The takbeer of these days — *Allahu Akbar Allahu Akbar, la ilaha illallah, Allahu Akbar Allahu Akbar wa lillahil hamd* — should be recited abundantly from 1 Dhul Hijjah until the last of the Tashreeq days.

---

## Part 8: Hajj and Umrah — Understanding the Difference

Many Muslims confuse Hajj and Umrah or believe performing one replaces the other.

| | Hajj | Umrah |
|---|---|---|
| Status | Obligatory (once) | Strongly recommended (Sunnah Mu'akkadah) |
| Timing | Only 8–13 Dhul Hijjah | Any time of year |
| Rituals | Ihram, Tawaf, Sa'i, Arafah, Muzdalifah, Jamarat, Sacrifice, Tawaf al-Ifadah | Ihram, Tawaf, Sa'i, Hair cut/shave |
| Duration | 5–6 days minimum | 2–3 hours to 1 day |
| Arafah | Required — Hajj is invalid without it | Not part of Umrah |
| Validity | Umrah does NOT fulfil Hajj obligation | Hajj does NOT replace Umrah |

Performing Umrah before Hajj (Hajj al-Tamattu) is the recommended combination for overseas pilgrims. Many Muslims perform Umrah in Ramadan before saving for Hajj — the Prophet ﷺ said that Umrah in Ramadan equals Hajj in reward (though not in obligation-fulfilment).

---

## The Return: Who You Will Be After Hajj

The Arabic term for a person who has completed Hajj is *Hajji* (male) or *Hajjah* (female). But the title is only meaningful if the journey's transformation endures.

The scholars say: the sign of an accepted Hajj is that the person returns *better* than they left. They pray with more presence. They are kinder. They are less attached to this world. They remember death more. They remember Allah more. Their priorities are rearranged.

This is not guaranteed — it must be worked for. The Prophet ﷺ said:

<HadithBlock
  text="Follow up the Hajj with Umrah, for those two remove poverty and sins just as bellows remove impurities from iron, gold, and silver. And an accepted Hajj has no reward except Paradise."
  narrator="Abdullah ibn Masud (رضي الله عنه)"
  source="Sunan al-Tirmidhi 810 — Authenticated by al-Albani"
/>

The reward of an accepted Hajj — *Hajj Mabrur* — is nothing less than Paradise. The criteria for Hajj Mabrur, according to the scholars: performed with correct intention (for Allah alone), performed according to the Prophet's ﷺ example, funded from halal earnings, and accompanied by good character throughout the journey.

Allah willing, Hajj 2026 will be yours. And if not this year — then the dua for it, made with sincerity, has already begun the journey.
